name: Bimini Road Full
description: The Bimini Road (also known as the Bimini Wall) is a submerged rock formation located off the coast of North Bimini Island in the Bahamas. Proponents of alternative history and pseudoarchaeology suggest it is a remnant of a paved road, wall, or other structure, potentially dating back to the lost city of Atlantis or another advanced, pre-Columbian civilization. These theories often stem from Edgar Cayce's prophecies, which predicted the rediscovery of Atlantis near Bimini in 1968 or 1969. The 'Full' designation in this context refers to the complete set of claims and interpretations associated with the site, encompassing its alleged artificial origins, connection to Atlantis, and implications for understanding human history. Key evidence cited includes the rectangular shape and arrangement of the stones, their relative flatness, and the apparent presence of mortar or cement in some areas. Mainstream geological explanations attribute the formation to natural processes of beachrock formation and erosion. Geologists argue that the 'paving stones' are beachrock slabs that fractured along natural joints, creating the appearance of a constructed road. Chemical analysis of the stones supports their composition as beachrock, a common coastal geological feature. Furthermore, no definitive archaeological evidence, such as tools or artifacts, has been found to substantiate claims of artificial construction. The debate continues, fueled by the visual appeal of the formation and the enduring fascination with Atlantis.
subtitle: Submerged stones: natural formation or ancient road?

claims.claim: The Bimini Road is an artificially constructed road or wall.
claims.evidence: Rectangular shape and arrangement of the stones; apparent flatness; alleged presence of mortar.
claims.criticism: Geological analysis indicates natural beachrock formation and fracturing; no definitive evidence of mortar; no artifacts found.

claims.claim: The Bimini Road is a remnant of the lost city of Atlantis.
claims.evidence: Edgar Cayce's prophecies; perceived similarities to descriptions of Atlantean architecture.
claims.criticism: Cayce's prophecies lack scientific basis; no verifiable connection to Atlantis established; location inconsistent with other Atlantean theories.

claims.claim: The Bimini Road proves the existence of a pre-Columbian advanced civilization in the Bahamas.
claims.evidence: Alleged sophistication of the 'construction'; potential age predating known indigenous cultures.
claims.criticism: No evidence of advanced technology or civilization found at the site; geological age consistent with natural formation processes.

keyFigures.name: Edgar Cayce
keyFigures.role: Whose prophecies sparked interest in Atlantis near Bimini

keyFigures.name: J. Manson Valentine
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who popularized the Bimini Road as a possible road

keyFigures.name: Eugene Shinn
keyFigures.role: Geologist who argued for a natural origin of the formation

timeline.event: Edgar Cayce makes prophecies about Atlantis rising near Bimini.
The Bimini Road is discovered and initially interpreted as a possible man-made structure.
Ongoing geological and archaeological investigations and debates about the formation's origin.

sources.author: Little, Greg
sources.work: The Bimini Road: An Archaeological Analysis
sources.title: Little, Greg
